'Ah!' -- the emotional exclamation that marks the volta. The certainty cracks.
'Mine eye may be deceiv'd' -- the honest admission. The Speaker's very organ of perception may be untrustworthy.
'Ere you were born was beauty's summer dead' -- the devastating close. Address the audience, not the FYM.

line_annotations.delivery_note: Stress 'To me' -- the qualification is everything. This is not a universal truth but a lover's confession.
line_annotations.gloss: In my eyes, dear friend, you can never grow old
line_annotations.commentary: 'To me' makes the claim perspectival from the start. The Speaker is not asserting an objective truth but an emotional one. 'Fair friend' is the warmest address in the sequence.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The eye/I pun is untranslatable and should be heard rather than explained. Let the repetition register.
line_annotations.gloss: For as you were when I first gazed upon you
line_annotations.commentary: 'When first your eye I ey'd' -- the pun on eye (organ) and I (self) collapses the boundary between seeing and being seen. The first gaze is the fixed reference point.

line_annotations.emotional_note: Counting, measuring despite the claim of changelessness
line_annotations.delivery_note: The counting begins. Each 'three' is a concession to time's passage that contradicts line 1.
line_annotations.gloss: Three cold winters have passed
line_annotations.commentary: The seasonal counting begins: three winters, three summers. The Speaker catalogues exactly the time he claims has had no effect, undermining his own assertion.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Beauteous springs to yellow autumn' is a decline from beauty to fading. Let the color shift register.
line_annotations.gloss: Three beautiful springs have turned to golden autumn
line_annotations.commentary: 'Three beauteous springs to yellow autumn turn'd' -- the transition from spring beauty to autumn yellowing is itself a figure for aging, making the counting-that-denies-change even more ironic.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Fresh' and 'green' are words of youth -- the Speaker reasserts the FYM's unchanged beauty after five lines of seasonal counting.
line_annotations.gloss: Since first I saw you looking fresh, and you still appear youthful
line_annotations.commentary: 'Fresh, which yet are green' -- the Speaker reasserts changelessness, but 'green' carries its own ambiguity: green as in youthful, but also unripe, naive.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Ah!' marks the turn. The dial-hand image should unfold slowly -- the comparison takes two lines to develop.
line_annotations.gloss: Ah, yet beauty moves like a clock's hour hand
line_annotations.commentary: The dial-hand metaphor: beauty moves like the hour hand of a clock -- imperceptibly but actually. The image is both beautiful and terrifying.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Steal' is the key verb -- beauty thieves away silently. 'No pace perceiv'd' -- the motion is real but invisible.
line_annotations.gloss: Stealing away from its own form, with no movement detected
line_annotations.commentary: 'Steal from his figure' -- beauty steals from its own shape/number on the dial. 'Figure' means both the body's form and the numeral on the clock face. 'No pace perceiv'd' -- the theft is too slow to see.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The admission of possible self-deception. This is the sonnet's bravest moment -- let it land quietly.
line_annotations.gloss: Has movement, and my eye may be deceived
line_annotations.commentary: 'Mine eye may be deceiv'd' -- the Speaker diagnoses his own perceptual bias. The lover's eye sees what it wants to see. This is remarkable self-awareness within a love poem.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The pivot to the future. 'Thou age unbred' addresses the reader -- break the fourth wall.
line_annotations.gloss: Fearing which, hear this, you unborn generation
line_annotations.commentary: 'Thou age unbred' -- the unborn future generation, which includes the reader. The Speaker turns from private love poem to public proclamation.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The final word is 'dead.' Let it fall with finality. The sonnet that began with 'never can be old' ends with death.
line_annotations.gloss: Before you were born, beauty's prime was already gone
line_annotations.commentary: The devastating close: from 'you never can be old' (line 1) to 'beauty's summer dead' (line 14). The arc of the sonnet is from denial to elegy. We, the readers, are too late.

character_appearances.evidence: Addressed as 'fair friend' (line 1) and 'you' throughout. The three-year marker gives the relationship a concrete history.
First-person throughout: 'To me,' 'I ey'd,' 'I seen,' 'mine eye.' The Speaker is both lover and self-diagnostician.

line_devices.quotation: when first your eye I ey'd
line_devices.explanation: Eye (organ of sight) and I (the self) collapse into each other. Seeing the FYM and being the Speaker are simultaneous acts.

line_devices.quotation: Three winters cold / Have from the forests shook three summers' pride
line_devices.explanation: The anaphoric 'three' (repeated in lines 3, 5, 7) counts the very time the Speaker claims has had no effect, creating an ironic counterpoint.

line_devices.quotation: beauty like a dial-hand
line_devices.explanation: Beauty compared to a clock's hour hand: moving constantly but too slowly to perceive. The simile captures time's stealth.

line_devices.quotation: Steal from his figure
line_devices.explanation: 'Figure' means both the body's form and the numeral on the clock face. Beauty steals away from both simultaneously.

line_devices.quotation: Steal from his figure, and no pace perceiv'd
line_devices.explanation: Time as thief -- beauty is stolen so gradually that the theft is invisible. The metaphor makes aging into a crime without witnesses.

line_devices.quotation: hear this thou age unbred
line_devices.explanation: Sudden address to unborn future generations -- the Speaker breaks from private love poem to prophetic public address.

line_devices.quotation: Ere you were born was beauty's summer dead
line_devices.explanation: The claim that beauty itself died with the FYM's youth is hyperbolic but emotionally devastating -- an elegy disguised as a warning.

line_devices.quotation: To me, fair friend, you never can be old
line_devices.explanation: Dramatic irony: the assertion of changelessness is undermined by 12 lines of seasonal counting and the admission that 'mine eye may be deceiv'd.'

modes.rationale: The sonnet enacts a psychological drama: the Speaker's confident assertion crumbles through self-examination into honest doubt and elegiac prophecy.
The dial-hand metaphor is one of the most famous images in the sequence -- its slow, imperceptible motion is the sonnet's imaginative center.
The time theme reaches its most sophisticated treatment: not time's violence but time's stealth, not destruction but imperceptible change.
The rhythmic regularity of the seasonal catalog (three...three...three) creates a metronomic effect that enacts the passage of time the Speaker denies.

analysis.argument_summary: The Speaker defends himself against the charge of idolatry: his love is not idolatrous because it is constant, devoted to 'one, of one, still such, and ever so.' The FYM embodies a trinity -- 'Fair, kind, and true' -- that gives the Speaker's repetitive praise its wondrous scope.
analysis.emotional_arc: denial of idolatry -> defense through constancy -> the trinitarian conceit -> epigrammatic assertion of uniqueness
analysis.dramatic_situation: Someone (perhaps the FYM himself, or a third party) has accused the Speaker of making the FYM an idol. The Speaker responds with a theological defense: his love is not idolatrous because it worships a trinity (fair, kind, true) that only the FYM unifies. The defense barely conceals that it confirms the charge.
analysis.subtext: The theological language is both playful and dangerous. In Protestant England, idolatry was a serious accusation. The Speaker's defense -- that his devotion is to a kind of Trinity -- is itself borderline blasphemous: he maps the FYM onto the three-in-one structure of the Christian Godhead. 'Fair, kind, and true' as three-in-one is an explicit Trinitarian structure. The Speaker is either brilliantly deflecting the charge or proving it.
analysis.analysis_text: Sonnet 105 is one of the most theologically provocative poems in the sequence, staging a defense against idolatry that barely conceals its own blasphemous implications. The opening imperative -- 'Let not my love be call'd idolatry' -- acknowledges that the charge has been made or anticipated. In Reformation England, where accusations of popish idolatry were both common and dangerous, the word carries political as well as theological weight. The Speaker's defense is paradoxical: his love is not idolatrous because it is monotheistic -- devoted 'To one, of one, still such, and ever so.' The repetitive simplicity of the line enacts the constancy it describes. The second quatrain develops the defense: the FYM's unchanging excellence ('Kind is my love to-day, to-morrow kind, / Still constant in a wondrous excellence') confines the Speaker's verse to constancy, leaving out 'difference.' This could be a limitation (the verse is monotonous) or a virtue (it reflects its unchanging subject). At line 9, the volta introduces the trinitarian conceit: 'Fair, kind, and true, is all my argument.' The three virtues become a refrain, repeated with variation across lines 9-10 and the couplet. The phrase 'Three themes in one, which wondrous scope affords' maps the FYM onto the Trinity: three persons in one God, three virtues in one beloved. The word 'wondrous' appears twice (lines 6 and 12), emphasizing the miraculous quality of this union. The couplet delivers the epigrammatic point: 'Fair, kind, and true, have often liv'd alone, / Which three till now, never kept seat in one.' The claim is that while fairness, kindness, and truth have existed separately, only the FYM combines all three. The phrase 'kept seat in one' suggests both sitting together (as in a throne or parliament) and dwelling in one body. The sonnet is a masterpiece of equivocation: the defense against idolatry is itself idolatrous, the denial of blasphemy is itself blasphemous, and the celebration of constancy is itself exquisitely varied in its repetitions of 'fair, kind, and true.'

directing.scene_setting: A public or semi-public moment: the Speaker defending his love against an accusation. The tone is courtroom defense mixed with theological disputation.
directing.character_note: The Speaker is a lawyer arguing his own case, and the defense is more incriminating than any prosecution. He knows this.
directing.fym_note: The FYM is the object of worship, now explicitly mapped onto the Trinity: fair (beauty), kind (generosity), true (faithfulness). The deification is complete.
directing.overall_arc: Open with the denial of idolatry, build the defense through constancy, introduce the trinitarian formula, and close with the epigrammatic claim of the FYM's uniqueness.

key_moments.note: 'Let not my love be call'd idolatry' -- the opening defense. The fact that it needs to be said means the charge has been made.
'To one, of one, still such, and ever so' -- the rhythmic monotony enacts the constancy it describes.
'Fair, kind, and true' -- the first statement of the trinitarian formula. It will be repeated three times, like a creed.
'Three themes in one, which wondrous scope affords' -- the Trinity mapping is explicit. Let the theological implication register.
'Which three till now, never kept seat in one' -- the epigrammatic close: the FYM is unprecedented. No one before combined all three.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The denial that confirms the charge. 'Idolatry' should land with its full theological weight.
line_annotations.gloss: Do not call my love idolatry
line_annotations.commentary: The opening imperative acknowledges the accusation. In Protestant England, idolatry was associated with Catholic image-worship -- to be called an idolater was a serious charge.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The line should sound like a prayer: repetitive, rhythmic, almost hypnotic. The monotony is the point.
line_annotations.gloss: To one person, about one person, always the same, forever
line_annotations.commentary: The line enacts constancy through repetition: 'one...one...still...ever.' The monotony is both a defense (I am constant) and a confession (I am obsessed).

line_annotations.delivery_note: The framing repetition of 'kind' (beginning and end) creates a verbal embrace.
line_annotations.gloss: My beloved is kind today and will be kind tomorrow
line_annotations.commentary: 'Kind' placed at the beginning and repeated at the end of the line creates a verbal frame: kindness contains the day. The word also carries its older sense of 'natural' -- the FYM is kind by nature.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Leaves out difference' -- the Speaker acknowledges the limitation of his constancy. There is wry self-knowledge here.
line_annotations.gloss: Expressing one thing only, leaving out variety
line_annotations.commentary: 'Leaves out difference' -- the verse's constancy excludes variety. This is simultaneously a defense (constancy is virtuous) and an admission (the poetry is repetitive).

line_annotations.delivery_note: The first statement of the trinitarian formula. Deliver it like a creed -- 'Fair, kind, and true' should sound like 'Father, Son, and Holy Ghost.'
line_annotations.gloss: 'Fair, kind, and true' is the entirety of my subject
line_annotations.commentary: The trinitarian formula appears for the first time. The quotation marks in some editions set it off as a creed or motto. The three virtues correspond roughly to Beauty, Goodness, and Truth -- a Platonic triad.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The repetition of the formula 'varying to other words' enacts what it describes: the same content in different form.
line_annotations.gloss: 'Fair, kind, and true,' rearranged in different expressions
line_annotations.commentary: The Speaker describes his poetic method: repeating 'fair, kind, and true' with variation. The line itself demonstrates the technique -- it repeats the formula while adding 'varying to other words.'

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Three themes in one' -- the Trinity mapping is explicit. Let the blasphemous implication register without overplaying it.
line_annotations.gloss: Three themes united in one person, which affords wondrous range
line_annotations.commentary: 'Three themes in one' maps directly onto the Trinity: three persons in one God, three virtues in one beloved. 'Wondrous scope' -- the unity-in-trinity paradoxically creates, not limits, poetic range.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The epigrammatic close: the FYM is unprecedented. 'Kept seat in one' should sound permanent and regal.
line_annotations.gloss: These three qualities have never before resided together in one person
line_annotations.commentary: 'Kept seat in one' -- the three virtues have never before shared a throne (seat) in one body. The image is both parliamentary (three members sharing one seat) and incarnational (three divine attributes in one person).

character_appearances.evidence: Referred to as 'my beloved' (line 2) and 'my love' (line 5). Not directly addressed but the object of the Speaker's devotion and defense.
First-person throughout, defending 'my love' against the charge of idolatry. The defense is self-revealing.

line_devices.quotation: Let not my love be call'd idolatry
line_devices.explanation: The defense against idolatry is itself idolatrous: the Speaker will proceed to map the FYM onto the Trinity, confirming rather than refuting the charge.

line_devices.quotation: To one, of one, still such, and ever so
line_devices.explanation: The repetitive structure (one...one...still...ever) creates a liturgical rhythm that enacts the constancy it describes.

line_devices.quotation: Fair, kind, and true, is all my argument
line_devices.explanation: The trinitarian conceit: three virtues in one beloved, mapping onto three persons in one God. The conceit governs the final six lines.

line_devices.quotation: Three themes in one, which wondrous scope affords
line_devices.explanation: The paradox of unity-in-trinity: three themes confined to one person should limit scope but instead create 'wondrous scope.'

line_devices.quotation: Fair, kind, and true
line_devices.explanation: The formula is repeated three times (lines 9, 10, 13) like a liturgical refrain or creed, reinforcing the trinitarian structure.

line_devices.quotation: Which three till now, never kept seat in one
line_devices.explanation: The epigrammatic close claims the FYM is unprecedented: no one before united all three virtues. The claim is both hyperbolic and creedal.

line_devices.quotation: One thing expressing, leaves out difference
line_devices.explanation: Constancy versus variety: expressing one thing means excluding difference. The antithesis is both a limitation and a virtue.

modes.rationale: The theological mapping of the FYM onto the Trinity is the sonnet's most distinctive and provocative contribution to the sequence.
The sonnet is structured as a legal/theological defense: accusation denied (Q1), constancy asserted (Q2), trinitarian formula introduced (Q3), uniqueness claimed (C).
The threefold repetition of 'Fair, kind, and true' creates a structural pattern that mirrors the trinitarian content.
The idolatry accusation carries specific weight in Reformation England, where Catholic image-worship was politically and theologically charged.

analysis.argument_summary: All beauty praised in past literature -- descriptions of fair ladies and lovely knights in old chronicles -- was unknowingly a prophecy of the FYM. Ancient poets had the vision to prefigure his beauty but lacked the skill to fully express it. We who see the FYM now have eyes to wonder but lack tongues to praise.
analysis.emotional_arc: survey of literary history -> recognition of past beauty as prophecy -> admission of present inadequacy
analysis.dramatic_situation: The Speaker reads old literature and recognizes in its descriptions of beauty an unconscious prophecy of the FYM. Every blazon of hand, foot, lip, eye, and brow was an attempt to describe what only the FYM fully embodies. But even the present generation, who actually see the FYM, cannot praise him adequately.
analysis.subtext: The sonnet makes a breathtaking historical claim: all beauty writing in literary history is retrospectively about the FYM. The final couplet's admission of inadequacy is bitterly ironic -- the Speaker, who has just demonstrated extraordinary praise, claims he cannot praise. The 'wasted time' of the opening line carries its double meaning: time that is past and time that is squandered.
analysis.analysis_text: Sonnet 106 offers the grandest temporal perspective in the sequence, sweeping from the 'chronicle of wasted time' through all literary history to the present moment. The opening image -- 'When in the chronicle of wasted time / I see descriptions of the fairest wights' -- positions the Speaker as a reader of old books, encountering descriptions of beauty that strike him as eerily familiar. 'Wasted time' means both past time (time that has elapsed) and time squandered (because it lacked the FYM to give it meaning). 'Wights' is deliberately archaic -- it belongs to the old chronicles the Speaker is reading. The second quatrain specifies the connection: 'Then, in the blazon of sweet beauty's best, / Of hand, of foot, of lip, of eye, of brow.' The catalog of body parts is a conventional blazon, but the Speaker sees in these ancient catalogs an attempt to describe the FYM: 'their antique pen would have express'd / Even such a beauty as you master now.' 'Would have express'd' is conditional and retrospective: the ancient poets wanted to describe this beauty but could only approximate it. At the volta, the implication is made explicit: 'So all their praises are but prophecies / Of this our time, all you prefiguring.' All past beauty writing is retroactively about the FYM. The word 'prefiguring' is theological -- in Christian typology, Old Testament events 'prefigure' New Testament fulfillments. The FYM is the fulfillment of all beauty's types. Lines 11-12 add a qualification: 'And for they looked but with divining eyes, / They had not skill enough your worth to sing.' The past poets had prophetic vision ('divining eyes') but not the craft to match it. The couplet completes the argument with devastating irony: 'For we, which now behold these present days, / Have eyes to wonder, but lack tongues to praise.' The present generation has direct sight of the FYM but still cannot praise him adequately. The sonnet that has just praised the FYM brilliantly across 14 lines ends by claiming such praise is impossible.

directing.scene_setting: The Speaker in a library or study, old books open before him, recognizing the FYM in every ancient description of beauty.
directing.character_note: The Speaker as literary historian: reading the past through the lens of his love. His claim is grand but sincere -- he genuinely sees the FYM in every old blazon.
directing.fym_note: The FYM is the fulfillment of all literary beauty. Every past description was a type of which he is the antitype. He masters a beauty that history could only prophesy.
directing.overall_arc: Begin in the library with old chronicles, build to the prophetic claim, and close with the ironic confession of present inadequacy.

key_moments.note: 'The chronicle of wasted time' -- open with sweep and melancholy. The Speaker surveys all of literary history.
'Then, in the blazon of sweet beauty's best' -- the blazon tradition invoked. The catalog of body parts should feel both conventional and revelatory.
'So all their praises are but prophecies' -- the grand claim. All beauty writing is about the FYM. Deliver with conviction.
'Have eyes to wonder, but lack tongues to praise' -- the ironic close. The Speaker who has just praised brilliantly claims he cannot praise.

line_annotations.delivery_note: Open with grandeur. 'The chronicle of wasted time' surveys all of history.
line_annotations.gloss: When I read the records of past ages
line_annotations.commentary: 'Chronicle of wasted time' -- 'wasted' means both elapsed and squandered. Time before the FYM is both past and purposeless. The word 'chronicle' suggests medieval histories.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Wights' is deliberately old-fashioned -- the Speaker is ventriloquizing the archaic diction of the chronicles he reads.
line_annotations.gloss: I see descriptions of the most beautiful people
line_annotations.commentary: 'Wights' (persons, beings) is an archaism even in Shakespeare's time, used deliberately to evoke the old books the Speaker is reading.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Blazon' is a technical term -- the catalog of beauties. The Speaker sees the convention and recognizes the FYM behind it.
line_annotations.gloss: Then, in the catalog of supreme beauty
line_annotations.commentary: 'Blazon' is the formal catalog of a beloved's beauties, feature by feature. The Speaker recognizes the FYM as the template behind every blazon ever written.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The five body parts should be listed with equal weight, like items in an inventory -- each one a piece of the FYM.
line_annotations.gloss: Of hand, of foot, of lip, of eye, of brow
line_annotations.commentary: The blazon catalog: five body parts in five prepositional phrases. The repetition of 'of' creates a rhythmic insistence. Each feature is a piece of beauty that the FYM unifies.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'You master now' -- the shift to the present tense and direct address is the sonnet's emotional peak.
line_annotations.gloss: A beauty exactly like the one you now possess
line_annotations.commentary: 'You master now' -- the FYM 'masters' beauty in the sense of possessing it and commanding it. The present tense breaks from the past-tense narrative of literary history.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The grand claim: all past praise was prophecy. 'Prophecies' should carry its full theological weight.
line_annotations.gloss: So all their praises were merely prophecies
line_annotations.commentary: 'Prophecies' imports the language of biblical typology: the past 'prefigures' the present, as the Old Testament prefigures the New. The FYM is the fulfillment.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Divining eyes' -- the past poets were seers, not merely artists. They saw the FYM without knowing it.
line_annotations.gloss: And because they looked only with prophetic vision
line_annotations.commentary: 'Divining eyes' -- eyes that divine (prophesy, perceive supernaturally). The past poets are credited with vision even as they are denied skill.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'We' -- the Speaker includes himself in the present generation's inadequacy. The first-person plural is a confession.
line_annotations.gloss: For we who now witness these present times
line_annotations.commentary: 'We, which now behold these present days' -- the Speaker pivots from past to present and includes himself in the collective. The present tense 'behold' contrasts with the past's 'divining.'

line_annotations.delivery_note: The final antithesis: eyes versus tongues, wonder versus praise. The sonnet that has praised brilliantly claims praise is impossible.
line_annotations.gloss: Have the eyes to marvel but lack the words to praise
line_annotations.commentary: The devastating ironic close: the present generation has direct perception ('eyes to wonder') but no adequate expression ('lack tongues to praise'). The sonnet itself disproves the claim.

character_appearances.evidence: Addressed as 'you' (lines 8, 10, 12). He is the fulfillment of all literary beauty, the antitype of every blazon.
First-person singular and plural: 'I see' (line 2), 'we' (line 13). The Speaker is reader, lover, and self-confessed inadequate praiser.

line_devices.quotation: the chronicle of wasted time
line_devices.explanation: 'Wasted' means both elapsed (time that has passed) and squandered (time without the FYM). The double meaning enriches the opening.

line_devices.quotation: Of hand, of foot, of lip, of eye, of brow
line_devices.explanation: The formal blazon catalog: five body parts in parallel prepositional phrases. The convention is named and enacted simultaneously.

line_devices.quotation: all their praises are but prophecies / Of this our time, all you prefiguring
line_devices.explanation: The typological conceit: all past beauty writing as Old Testament prophecy, the FYM as New Testament fulfillment. Literary history becomes sacred history.

line_devices.quotation: they looked but with divining eyes
line_devices.explanation: The past poets as prophets with 'divining eyes' -- they saw the future FYM but dimly, as through a glass darkly.

line_devices.quotation: Have eyes to wonder, but lack tongues to praise
line_devices.explanation: Eyes versus tongues, perception versus expression. The present generation can see but not speak. The antithesis structures the sonnet's conclusion.

line_devices.quotation: Have eyes to wonder, but lack tongues to praise
line_devices.explanation: The irony of a sonnet that praises brilliantly while claiming praise is impossible. The very poem disproves its own final claim.

line_devices.quotation: Of hand, of foot, of lip, of eye, of brow
line_devices.explanation: The fivefold repetition of 'of' creates a rhythmic catalog that enacts the blazon convention while surveying the body.

modes.rationale: The claim that all literary history is a prophecy of the FYM is the sonnet's most ambitious thematic gesture.
The sonnet explicitly engages with literary tradition: chronicles, blazons, antique pens. It is a poem about reading and recognizing the beloved in all past writing.
The argument builds from observation (I see past descriptions) through claim (they were prophecies) to conclusion (we cannot praise adequately). The logic is elegant if hyperbolic.
The references to 'chronicles,' 'antique pen,' 'ladies dead and lovely knights' evoke a specific literary-historical landscape: medieval romance and chronicle poetry.

analysis.argument_summary: Neither the Speaker's private fears nor the world's prophetic anxieties can control the lease of his love. The 'mortal moon' has survived her eclipse, uncertainties are resolved, and peace reigns. In this calm, the Speaker's love is renewed, and his verse will outlast tyrants and monuments.
analysis.emotional_arc: fear and uncertainty -> historical crisis resolved -> personal and poetic renewal -> defiant prophecy of permanence
analysis.dramatic_situation: A historical crisis has passed -- most scholars identify the 'mortal moon' with Queen Elizabeth I and her 'eclipse' with either her death (1603) or her survival of a feared event. The resolution of this crisis renews both the Speaker's love and his confidence in poetry's immortalizing power.
analysis.subtext: This is one of the most debated sonnets in the sequence because it appears to reference a specific historical event. If the 'mortal moon' is Elizabeth and the 'eclipse' her death in 1603, the sonnet dates itself and possibly the entire sequence. The 'olives of endless age' suggest the peace that followed James I's accession. But the personal and political intertwine: the national crisis resolving parallels the lover's fears about the relationship resolving. Public history and private love are made to rhyme.
analysis.analysis_text: Sonnet 107 is the most historically specific poem in the sequence, and its references have generated more scholarly debate than perhaps any other sonnet. The opening quatrain establishes a framework of fear: 'Not mine own fears, nor the prophetic soul / Of the wide world dreaming on things to come' -- both private anxiety and collective apocalyptic dread. The love's 'lease' is 'supposed as forfeit to a confin'd doom' -- the relationship was thought to be under a death sentence, constrained by some limiting fate. Lines 5-8 announce the resolution of the crisis. 'The mortal moon hath her eclipse endur'd' is the crux: most scholars read 'mortal moon' as Elizabeth I (the Virgin Queen, associated with Diana/the moon), and 'eclipse' as either her death or her survival of a crisis. 'Endur'd' is ambiguous -- it can mean 'survived' or 'undergone.' The 'sad augurs' who predicted doom now 'mock their own presage' -- the prophecies of disaster were wrong. 'Incertainties now crown themselves assur'd' -- a line whose meaning depends on the historical event, but which captures the psychological relief of crisis passed. 'Peace proclaims olives of endless age' -- if this is 1603, it refers to the peaceful accession of James I. At line 9, the volta turns from public history to personal renewal: 'Now with the drops of this most balmy time, / My love looks fresh.' The historical resolution renews the private love. 'Death to me subscribes' -- Death yields, submits to the Speaker, because poetry will outlive him. The couplet delivers the immortality claim with full confidence: 'And thou in this shalt find thy monument, / When tyrants' crests and tombs of brass are spent.' The poem itself is the FYM's monument, more durable than the heraldic crests and brass tombs of tyrants. The word 'spent' echoes the economic language of the sequence: tyrants' monuments will be exhausted, used up, while the poem endures.

directing.scene_setting: A public moment: a national crisis has resolved. The Speaker feels the collective relief and channels it into personal and poetic renewal.
directing.character_note: The Speaker is at his most historically aware and publicly confident. This is not the private lover but the poet claiming a place in history.
directing.fym_note: The FYM is addressed in the couplet ('thou'). He will find his monument in this verse. The crisis has renewed rather than destroyed the relationship.
directing.overall_arc: Open with accumulated fears (private and public), announce the resolution at line 5, turn to personal renewal at line 9, and close with the monumental claim.

key_moments.note: 'Not mine own fears' -- begin with the negative. The fears existed but have been overcome.
'The mortal moon hath her eclipse endur'd' -- the historical crux. Deliver with relief and awe.
'Incertainties now crown themselves assur'd' -- the paradox of uncertainty becoming certainty. The political crisis resolving.
'Now with the drops of this most balmy time' -- the turn to personal renewal. The 'now' is emphatic.
'When tyrants' crests and tombs of brass are spent' -- the defiant close. Poetry outlasts power.

line_annotations.delivery_note: Open with the weight of accumulated dread. 'Prophetic soul' has an ominous ring.
line_annotations.gloss: Neither my personal fears nor the prophetic spirit
line_annotations.commentary: The opening distinguishes private fears from collective ones. 'The prophetic soul / Of the wide world' is the collective unconscious -- the world's dim awareness of approaching crisis.

line_annotations.delivery_note: Legal language: forfeit, lease, doom. The love was under sentence.
line_annotations.gloss: Thought to be forfeited to a limited, predetermined fate
line_annotations.commentary: 'Forfeit to a confin'd doom' -- the love's lease thought to be cancelled by a predetermined fate. 'Confin'd' means limited, bounded -- the doom has an expiration date.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The line that has launched a thousand scholarly arguments. Deliver with the gravity of a historical turning point.
line_annotations.gloss: The mortal moon has survived her eclipse
line_annotations.commentary: 'The mortal moon' -- most likely Elizabeth I, associated with Diana/the moon. 'Eclipse endur'd' -- survived (or undergone) her crisis. The line dates the sonnet if we can identify the event.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The paradox of uncertainty crowning itself with assurance. The political and emotional relief are one.
line_annotations.gloss: Uncertainties now declare themselves resolved
line_annotations.commentary: 'Crown themselves assur'd' -- the metaphor of self-coronation suggests a political accession. Uncertainties becoming certainties is both political (succession resolved) and personal (love renewed).

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Olives of endless age' -- the olive branch of peace. The optimism is possibly naive but emotionally necessary.
line_annotations.gloss: And peace proclaims an era of everlasting harmony
line_annotations.commentary: 'Olives of endless age' -- the olive branch as emblem of peace. If this is 1603, it refers to James I's peace policy. 'Endless age' is optimistic hyperbole.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Now' is emphatic -- the crisis is past, the renewal is here. 'Balmy' (healing, soothing) marks the turn to gentleness.
line_annotations.gloss: Now in this most healing season
line_annotations.commentary: 'Balmy time' -- 'balmy' means both fragrant and healing (from 'balm'). The historical crisis resolved creates a personal springtime.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Death to me subscribes' -- Death yields, submits. The Speaker has won a personal victory over mortality.
line_annotations.gloss: My love appears renewed, and Death yields to me
line_annotations.commentary: 'Death to me subscribes' -- subscribes means submits, yields authority. Death acknowledges the Speaker's verse as a superior force. The claim is bold even by this sequence's standards.

line_annotations.emotional_note: Prophetic, intimate within the public claim
line_annotations.delivery_note: The pivot back to the FYM: 'thou in this' -- this poem is your monument. The public historical claim narrows to private address.
line_annotations.gloss: And you will find your memorial in this poem
line_annotations.commentary: 'Thou in this' -- the FYM's monument is the sonnet itself. The shift from public history to private address is characteristic of the sequence's interweaving of the personal and the historical.

line_annotations.delivery_note: End with the contrast: tyrants' monuments will be 'spent' (exhausted, used up), but the poem endures.
line_annotations.gloss: When the heraldic emblems and brass tombs of tyrants are exhausted
line_annotations.commentary: 'Tyrants' crests and tombs of brass' -- the most durable monuments of power. 'Spent' means exhausted, used up -- the economic language makes permanence a resource that even brass runs out of.

character_appearances.evidence: Addressed as 'thou' in line 13: 'thou in this shalt find thy monument.' The FYM is the private beneficiary of the public-historical claim.
First-person: 'mine own fears,' 'my true love,' 'to me subscribes,' 'I'll live.' The Speaker claims both personal and poetic triumph.

line_devices.quotation: The mortal moon hath her eclipse endur'd
line_devices.explanation: Elizabeth I as the 'mortal moon' (Diana, virginity, changeability). The eclipse is the crisis -- death or danger -- that has been survived or undergone.

line_devices.quotation: Incertainties now crown themselves assur'd
line_devices.explanation: Uncertainties becoming certainties is paradoxical: self-crowning transforms doubt into authority. The political succession metaphor is embedded.

line_devices.quotation: peace proclaims olives of endless age
line_devices.explanation: Peace personified as a herald making a proclamation. The olive branch is ceremonially offered as an emblem of the new era.

line_devices.quotation: Death to me subscribes
line_devices.explanation: Death personified as a subordinate who 'subscribes' (signs under, yields authority) to the Speaker's poetic power.

line_devices.quotation: When tyrants' crests and tombs of brass are spent
line_devices.explanation: The claim that the poem will outlast even brass monuments. The hyperbole serves the immortality-through-verse theme.

line_devices.quotation: the lease of my true love control
line_devices.explanation: Love as a lease -- a legal contract with an expiration date. The fears and prophecies would 'control' (terminate, constrain) this lease.

line_devices.quotation: While he insults o'er dull and speechless tribes
line_devices.explanation: Death 'insults' (triumphs over) those who left no verse -- the 'dull and speechless tribes.' The antithesis: poetry speaks; the unpoetic are silenced by death.

modes.rationale: The sonnet is the most historically specific in the sequence, apparently referencing a datable political event. The historical mode is dominant.
The immortality-through-verse theme reaches its most confident expression: Death subscribes, tyrants' monuments are spent, but the poem endures.
The argument moves from negation (not my fears, nor the world's) through historical resolution to personal-poetic triumph. The structure is climactic.
The images are political and monumental: the mortal moon, olives of endless age, tyrants' crests and brass tombs.

analysis.argument_summary: The Speaker has nothing new to say -- every thought has already been expressed. But love, like prayer, sanctifies repetition. The act of saying 'thou mine, I thine' daily is not staleness but ritual, and eternal love makes repetition sacred rather than tedious.
analysis.emotional_arc: confession of having nothing new to say -> acceptance that love requires no novelty -> elevation of repetition to prayer -> assertion that love defeats time through ritual
analysis.dramatic_situation: The Speaker confronts the exhaustion of his poetic resources: what can he possibly write about the FYM that he has not already written? His answer is that love does not require novelty -- like daily prayer, the repetition of 'thou mine, I thine' is itself a form of devotion that transcends staleness.
analysis.subtext: The sonnet is profoundly self-aware about its position in a long sequence. After 107 sonnets, the Speaker admits he is repeating himself -- and transforms that admission into a virtue. The prayer analogy is the key: religious devotion does not require new words each day, and neither does love. The phrase 'sweet boy' is one of the most intimate forms of address in the sequence, carrying tenderness and an awareness of the age difference between Speaker and FYM.
analysis.analysis_text: Sonnet 108 confronts the problem of poetic exhaustion with disarming honesty and transforms it into a statement about the nature of love itself. The opening questions -- 'What's in the brain, that ink may character, / Which hath not figur'd to thee my true spirit?' -- admit that the Speaker has already expressed everything expressible. 'Character' means both to write (inscribe characters) and to represent. 'Figur'd' means both depicted and prefigured. The first quatrain catalogs the impossibility of novelty: what is new to speak, new to register, that might express love or merit? The answer comes at line 5 with devastating simplicity: 'Nothing, sweet boy.' The address 'sweet boy' is startling in its intimacy -- one of only two uses in the Sonnets (the other is 126). It acknowledges the age gap and carries a tenderness that 'fair friend' or 'my love' do not. The prayer analogy follows immediately: 'like prayers divine, / I must each day say o'er the very same.' The comparison of love poetry to prayer is the sonnet's master stroke. Prayer does not require novelty; the Pater Noster is said daily without loss of meaning. The ritual repetition ('Counting no old thing old') sanctifies what would otherwise be tedium. 'Thou mine, I thine' is the simplest possible expression of mutual possession -- a vow repeated daily like a marriage oath. Line 8's 'Even as when first I hallow'd thy fair name' imports the language of consecration: 'hallow'd' means made holy, set apart as sacred. The FYM's name was hallowed at first speaking and remains hallowed through repetition. The sestet (lines 9-14) elevates the argument: 'eternal love in love's fresh case / Weighs not the dust and injury of age.' Eternal love in a fresh container -- the 'case' is both a covering and a legal case, a fresh argument for an old truth. Love does not weigh (consider, regard) the damage of aging. It 'makes antiquity for aye his page' -- turns old age into a servant ('page') forever. The couplet resolves with quiet triumph: love finds its original impulse ('the first conceit of love there bred') still alive in a place where time and external appearance would declare it dead. The sonnet is a fitting near-conclusion to this group: the Speaker has nothing new but discovers that newness was never the point.

directing.scene_setting: An intimate, almost devotional moment. The Speaker is not performing but praying -- repeating the words of love as a daily office.
directing.character_note: The Speaker is at his most mature and tender. The admission of having nothing new to say is not defeat but a deeper understanding of love's nature.
directing.fym_note: Addressed as 'sweet boy' -- one of the most intimate moments in the entire sequence. The FYM is both beloved and object of devotion, like a saint to whom prayers are addressed.
directing.overall_arc: Open with the honest confession of exhaustion, land 'Nothing, sweet boy' as the emotional center, develop the prayer analogy, and close with the quiet assertion that love finds itself alive where time would declare it dead.

key_moments.note: 'What's in the brain, that ink may character' -- open with genuine bewilderment. The poet is at a loss.
'Nothing, sweet boy' -- the most intimate moment. The pause after 'Nothing' should be felt. Then the tenderness of 'sweet boy.'
'Counting no old thing old, thou mine, I thine' -- the mutual possession vow. Simple as a wedding oath.
'Even as when first I hallow'd thy fair name' -- 'hallow'd' carries its full sacred weight.
'Where time and outward form would show it dead' -- the final word is 'dead,' but the meaning is life. Love survives where time says it should not.

line_annotations.delivery_note: Open with genuine questioning. The poet is searching his mind and finding nothing new.
line_annotations.gloss: What thought in the brain can ink set down in writing
line_annotations.commentary: 'Character' as a verb means to inscribe, write. The question is whether anything remains in the mind that has not already been written. The answer will be no.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'True spirit' is what the Speaker has been trying to convey all along. Has he succeeded? The question is genuine.
line_annotations.gloss: That has not already represented my true feelings to you?
line_annotations.commentary: 'Figur'd' means both depicted and prefigured. 'True spirit' -- the authentic self that poetry attempts to transmit. The Speaker wonders if the transmission is complete.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The question trails off: what more can express love or describe your merit? The rhetorical questions are genuine, not merely formal.
line_annotations.gloss: That might express my love or your precious worth?
line_annotations.commentary: 'Dear merit' -- 'dear' means both beloved and valuable. The FYM's worth is both emotional and quasi-economic, echoing the sequence's persistent commercial language.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The most important line in the sonnet. 'Nothing' answers the four questions of Q1. Then 'sweet boy' -- the tenderest address in the sequence. Then the pivot to prayer.
line_annotations.gloss: Nothing, dear boy; but yet, like sacred prayers
line_annotations.commentary: 'Nothing, sweet boy' is one of the sequence's most emotionally charged moments. 'Sweet boy' acknowledges youth, tenderness, and the age gap. The semicolon is a hinge: from despair ('Nothing') to devotion ('like prayers divine').

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Must' is obligation, not choice. The daily repetition is a duty and a privilege.
line_annotations.gloss: I must repeat the very same words each day
line_annotations.commentary: 'Must each day say o'er the very same' -- the compulsion of devotion. Like monastic hours, the love-prayer must be repeated daily. 'Must' makes it obligation, not mere habit.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Thou mine, I thine' is a marriage vow in miniature. Let the simplicity carry the weight.
line_annotations.gloss: Considering nothing old to be outdated -- you are mine, I am yours
line_annotations.commentary: 'Thou mine, I thine' -- the simplest expression of mutual love in the entire sequence. It echoes the Song of Songs and the marriage vow. 'Counting no old thing old' -- refusing to let repetition age the sentiment.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Hallow'd' is the word of the Lord's Prayer. The FYM's name was made holy at first speaking and remains so.
line_annotations.gloss: Just as when I first consecrated your beautiful name
line_annotations.commentary: 'Hallow'd' -- made holy, consecrated. The echo of 'hallowed be thy Name' in the Lord's Prayer is unmistakable. The FYM's name is a sacred word that gains power through repetition.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The shift from prayer to philosophy. 'Eternal love in love's fresh case' -- the old content in a new container.
line_annotations.gloss: So that eternal love, in love's renewed expression
line_annotations.commentary: 'Eternal love in love's fresh case' -- love that is eternal dressed in fresh expression. 'Case' means both container and legal argument: the same love, freshly cased/argued.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Makes antiquity for aye his page' -- old age becomes love's servant forever. The pun on 'page' should register.
line_annotations.gloss: But makes old age forever its servant
line_annotations.commentary: 'Page' puns: (1) a servant/attendant; (2) a page of a book. Love makes old age serve it -- or turns old age into a page of the book in which love is written.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The final word is 'dead,' but the meaning is life. Love finds itself alive precisely where time declares it should be dead.
line_annotations.gloss: Where time and outward appearance would declare it dead
line_annotations.commentary: The sonnet ends with 'dead' -- but the sentence's meaning is resurrection. Love finds its first impulse alive in a place where time and appearances say it should have died. The contradiction is the point.

character_appearances.evidence: Addressed as 'sweet boy' (line 5) and 'thee/thou/thy' throughout. The most intimate form of address in this group.
First-person: 'my true spirit,' 'my love,' 'I must,' 'I thine.' The Speaker is both devotee and self-diagnosed repetitive praiser.

line_devices.quotation: like prayers divine, / I must each day say o'er the very same
line_devices.explanation: Love poetry compared to prayer: both require daily repetition, and the repetition is the devotion, not a failure of invention.

line_devices.quotation: thou mine, I thine
line_devices.explanation: The chiastic structure (thou-mine / I-thine) creates a mirror image of mutual possession, each half reflecting the other.

line_devices.quotation: when first I hallow'd thy fair name
line_devices.explanation: The FYM's name hallowed (made sacred) like God's name in the Lord's Prayer. Love is worship; the beloved's name is a holy word.

line_devices.quotation: makes antiquity for aye his page
line_devices.explanation: 'Page' means both servant/attendant and the page of a book. Love conscripts old age as its servant and inscribes itself on time's pages.

line_devices.quotation: makes antiquity for aye his page
line_devices.explanation: Antiquity (old age) personified as a servant ('page') forced to attend upon love. Love commands time rather than submitting to it.

line_devices.quotation: Finding the first conceit of love there bred, / Where time and outward form would show it dead
line_devices.explanation: Love found alive where time declares it dead. The paradox of love's persistence in the face of physical decay.

line_devices.quotation: What's in the brain, that ink may character
line_devices.explanation: The brain as a text that ink might transcribe. 'Character' (write in characters) makes thought into inscription.

line_devices.quotation: eternal love in love's fresh case
line_devices.explanation: 'Case' as both container and legal argument: eternal love freshly encased, or the same love newly argued. The old content in a new vessel.

modes.rationale: The sonnet's central insight -- that love, like prayer, sanctifies repetition -- is its most profound thematic contribution.
The dramatic moment of 'Nothing, sweet boy' is the sonnet's emotional center -- the poet admitting exhaustion and then finding renewal in ritual.
The four rhetorical questions of Q1 set up the devastating answer of line 5, and the prayer analogy resolves what seemed like an impasse.
The prayer analogy invokes liturgical tradition; 'thou mine, I thine' echoes the Song of Songs; 'hallow'd' echoes the Lord's Prayer.
